revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: 「[[File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|thumb||270px|東照宮]] '''長崎東照宮'''（ながさきとうしょうぐう<ref group=注釈>正式名称は「東照宮」であり、地名を冠した「長崎東照宮」は通称。</ref>）は、[[長崎市|長崎]]に勧請された[[東照宮]]。 鎮座地は長崎市上西山町。社殿は3間1戸の素木による[[入母屋造]]で、正面には石鳥居がある。境内の一角には、神社庁|…」

revision.text: [[ja:長崎東照宮]]

[[ja:長崎東照宮]] {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = {{ill|Nagasaki City, Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a {{ill|Toshogu Shrine|ja|東照宮|lt=Toshogu Shrine}} enshrined in {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}.
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho}}, {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}. The shrine building is built in a {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|ja|鎮西大社諏訪神社|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦|lt=Hōreki era}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun era|ja|寛文|lt=Kanbun era}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakaue Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|lt=Kōnin era}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Shinjūji|ja|神宮寺|lt=Shinjūji}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunwa era|ja|文和|lt=Bunwa era}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄|lt=Eiroku era}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩|lt=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki Magistrates|ja|長崎奉行|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|ja|勝海舟|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://nagasakitoshogu.jp/ Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Shimbutsu Project] - A project on {{ill|Suwa faith|ja|諏訪信仰|lt=Suwa faith}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu Shūgō|ja|神仏習合|lt=Shinbutsu Shūgō}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri}} history.

In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>

== History == In {{ill|Kanbun era|ja|寛文|lt=Kanbun era}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakaue Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|lt=Kōnin era}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Shinjūji|ja|神宮寺|lt=Shinjūji}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunwa era|ja|文和|lt=Bunwa era}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄|lt=Eiroku era}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩|lt=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki Magistrates|ja|長崎奉行|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|ja|勝海舟|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

[[ja:長崎東照宮]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = {{ill|Nagasaki City, Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a {{ill|Toshogu Shrine|ja|東照宮|lt=Toshogu Shrine}} enshrined in {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}.
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho}}, {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}. The shrine building is built in a {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|ja|鎮西大社諏訪神社|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦|lt=Hōreki era}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun era|ja|寛文|lt=Kanbun era}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakaue Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|lt=Kōnin era}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Shinjūji|ja|神宮寺|lt=Shinjūji}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunwa era|ja|文和|lt=Bunwa era}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄|lt=Eiroku era}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩|lt=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki Magistrates|ja|長崎奉行|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|ja|勝海舟|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

revision.text:
[[ja:長崎東照宮]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = {{ill|Nagasaki City, Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a {{ill|Toshogu Shrine|ja|東照宮|lt=Toshogu Shrine}} enshrined in {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}.
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho}}, {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}. The shrine building is built in a {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|ja|鎮西大社諏訪神社|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦|lt=Hōreki era}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun era|ja|寛文|lt=Kanbun era}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakaue Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|lt=Kōnin era}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Shinjūji|ja|神宮寺|lt=Shinjūji}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunwa era|ja|文和|lt=Bunwa era}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄|lt=Eiroku era}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩|lt=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki Magistrates|ja|長崎奉行|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|ja|勝海舟|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

[[ja:長崎東照宮]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = {{ill|Nagasaki City, Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a {{ill|Toshogu Shrine|ja|東照宮|lt=Toshogu Shrine}} enshrined in {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}.
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho}}, {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}. The shrine building is built in a {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|ja|鎮西大社諏訪神社|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦|lt=Hōreki era}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun era|ja|寛文|lt=Kanbun era}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakaue Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|lt=Kōnin era}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Shinjūji|ja|神宮寺|lt=Shinjūji}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunwa era|ja|文和|lt=Bunwa era}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄|lt=Eiroku era}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩|lt=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki Magistrates|ja|長崎奉行|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|ja|勝海舟|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://nagasakitoshogu.jp/ Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Shimbutsu Project] - A project on {{ill|Suwa faith|ja|諏訪信仰|lt=Suwa faith}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu Shūgō|ja|神仏習合|lt=Shinbutsu Shūgō}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri}} history.
{{Shinto Navbox}} {{Shinto-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine}} [[Category:Tōshō-gū]] [[Category:Shrines in Nagasaki City]] [[Category:History of Nagasaki City]] [[Category:Edo period in Japan]] [[Category:Original pages]]

In {{ill|Bunwa era|ja|文和|lt=Bunwa era}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄|lt=Eiroku era}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩|lt=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki Magistrates|ja|長崎奉行|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|ja|勝海舟|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

[[ja:長崎東照宮]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = {{ill|Nagasaki City, Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a {{ill|Toshogu Shrine|ja|東照宮|lt=Toshogu Shrine}} enshrined in {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}.
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho}}, {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}. The shrine building is built in a {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|ja|鎮西大社諏訪神社|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦|lt=Hōreki era}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun era|ja|寛文|lt=Kanbun era}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakaue Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|lt=Kōnin era}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Shinjūji|ja|神宮寺|lt=Shinjūji}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunwa era|ja|文和|lt=Bunwa era}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄|lt=Eiroku era}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩|lt=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki Magistrates|ja|長崎奉行|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|ja|勝海舟|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

{{DEFAULTSORT:Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine}} [[Category:Tōshō-gū]] [[Category:Shrines in Nagasaki City]] [[Category:History of Nagasaki City]] [[Category:Edo period in Japan]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump-01-18]]

[[ja:長崎東照宮]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = {{ill|Nagasaki City, Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a {{ill|Toshogu Shrine|ja|東照宮|lt=Toshogu Shrine}} enshrined in {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}.
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho}}, {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}. The shrine building is built in a {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|ja|鎮西大社諏訪神社|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦|lt=Hōreki era}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun era|ja|寛文|lt=Kanbun era}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakaue Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|lt=Kōnin era}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Shinjūji|ja|神宮寺|lt=Shinjūji}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunwa era|ja|文和|lt=Bunwa era}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄|lt=Eiroku era}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩|lt=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki Magistrates|ja|長崎奉行|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|ja|勝海舟|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|ja|鎮西大社諏訪神社|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦|lt=Hōreki era}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun era|ja|寛文|lt=Kanbun era}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakaue Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|lt=Kōnin era}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Shinjūji|ja|神宮寺|lt=Shinjūji}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunwa era|ja|文和|lt=Bunwa era}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄|lt=Eiroku era}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩|lt=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki Magistrates|ja|長崎奉行|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|ja|勝海舟|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

[[ja:長崎東照宮]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = {{ill|Nagasaki City, Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a {{ill|Toshogu Shrine|ja|東照宮|lt=Toshogu Shrine}} enshrined in {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}.
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho}}, {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}. The shrine building is built in a {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|ja|鎮西大社諏訪神社|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦|lt=Hōreki era}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun era|ja|寛文|lt=Kanbun era}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakaue Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|lt=Kōnin era}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Shinjūji|ja|神宮寺|lt=Shinjūji}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunwa era|ja|文和|lt=Bunwa era}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄|lt=Eiroku era}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩|lt=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki Magistrates|ja|長崎奉行|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|ja|勝海舟|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

revision.text:
[[ja:長崎東照宮]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = {{ill|Nagasaki City, Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a {{ill|Toshogu Shrine|ja|東照宮|lt=Toshogu Shrine}} enshrined in {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}.
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho}}, {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}. The shrine building is built in a {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|ja|鎮西大社諏訪神社|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦|lt=Hōreki era}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun era|ja|寛文|lt=Kanbun era}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakaue Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|lt=Kōnin era}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Shinjūji|ja|神宮寺|lt=Shinjūji}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunwa era|ja|文和|lt=Bunwa era}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄|lt=Eiroku era}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩|lt=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki Magistrates|ja|長崎奉行|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|ja|勝海舟|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

revision.text:
[[ja:長崎東照宮]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = {{ill|Nagasaki City, Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a {{ill|Toshogu Shrine|ja|東照宮|lt=Toshogu Shrine}} enshrined in {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}.
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho}}, {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}. The shrine building is built in a {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|ja|鎮西大社諏訪神社|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦|lt=Hōreki era}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun era|ja|寛文|lt=Kanbun era}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakaue Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|lt=Kōnin era}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Shinjūji|ja|神宮寺|lt=Shinjūji}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunwa era|ja|文和|lt=Bunwa era}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄|lt=Eiroku era}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩|lt=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki Magistrates|ja|長崎奉行|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|ja|勝海舟|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

[[ja:長崎東照宮]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = {{ill|Nagasaki City, Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a {{ill|Toshogu Shrine|ja|東照宮|lt=Toshogu Shrine}} enshrined in {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}.
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho}}, {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}. The shrine building is built in a {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|ja|鎮西大社諏訪神社|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦|lt=Hōreki era}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun era|ja|寛文|lt=Kanbun era}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakaue Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|lt=Kōnin era}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Shinjūji|ja|神宮寺|lt=Shinjūji}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunwa era|ja|文和|lt=Bunwa era}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄|lt=Eiroku era}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩|lt=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki Magistrates|ja|長崎奉行|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|ja|勝海舟|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

== References == {{Reflist}}

[[ja:長崎東照宮]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = {{ill|Nagasaki City, Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a {{ill|Toshogu Shrine|ja|東照宮|lt=Toshogu Shrine}} enshrined in {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}.
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho}}, {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}. The shrine building is built in a {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|ja|鎮西大社諏訪神社|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦|lt=Hōreki era}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun era|ja|寛文|lt=Kanbun era}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakaue Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|lt=Kōnin era}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Shinjūji|ja|神宮寺|lt=Shinjūji}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunwa era|ja|文和|lt=Bunwa era}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄|lt=Eiroku era}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩|lt=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki Magistrates|ja|長崎奉行|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|ja|勝海舟|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://nagasakitoshogu.jp/ Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Shimbutsu Project] - A project on {{ill|Suwa faith|ja|諏訪信仰|lt=Suwa faith}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu Shūgō|ja|神仏習合|lt=Shinbutsu Shūgō}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri}} history.

[[ja:長崎東照宮]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = {{ill|Nagasaki City, Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a {{ill|Toshogu Shrine|ja|東照宮|lt=Toshogu Shrine}} enshrined in {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}.
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho}}, {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}. The shrine building is built in a {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|ja|鎮西大社諏訪神社|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦|lt=Hōreki era}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun era|ja|寛文|lt=Kanbun era}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakaue Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|lt=Kōnin era}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Shinjūji|ja|神宮寺|lt=Shinjūji}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunwa era|ja|文和|lt=Bunwa era}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄|lt=Eiroku era}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩|lt=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki Magistrates|ja|長崎奉行|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|ja|勝海舟|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>
