At {{ill|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|ja|鎮西大社諏訪神社|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦|lt=Hōreki era}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun era|ja|寛文|lt=Kanbun era}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakaue Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|lt=Kōnin era}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Shinjūji|ja|神宮寺|lt=Shinjūji}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunwa era|ja|文和|lt=Bunwa era}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄|lt=Eiroku era}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩|lt=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki Magistrates|ja|長崎奉行|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|ja|勝海舟|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

[[ja:長崎東照宮]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = {{ill|Nagasaki City, Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a {{ill|Toshogu Shrine|ja|東照宮|lt=Toshogu Shrine}} enshrined in {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}.
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho}}, {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City}}. The shrine building is built in a {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|ja|鎮西大社諏訪神社|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦|lt=Hōreki era}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = {{ill|Nagasaki City, Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|12=draft}}, Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|12=draft}} | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a {{ill|Toshogu Shrine|ja|東照宮|lt=Toshogu Shrine|12=draft}} enshrined in {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City|12=draft}}.
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City|12=draft}}. The shrine building is built in a {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|12=draft}} style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|12=draft}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|ja|鎮西大社諏訪神社|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|12=draft}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦|lt=Hōreki era|12=draft}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun era|ja|寛文|lt=Kanbun era|12=draft}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakaue Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro|12=draft}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=draft}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|12=draft}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=draft}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|lt=Suwa Taisha|12=draft}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|lt=Kōnin era|12=draft}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|lt=Kūkai|12=draft}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Shinjūji|ja|神宮寺|lt=Shinjūji|12=draft}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗|lt=Shingon Buddhism|12=draft}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=draft}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho|12=draft}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunwa era|ja|文和|lt=Bunwa era|12=draft}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi|12=draft}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄|lt=Eiroku era|12=draft}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi|12=draft}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩|lt=Suwa Domain|12=draft}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō|12=draft}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki Magistrates|ja|長崎奉行|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates|12=draft}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|ja|勝海舟|lt=Katsu Kaishū|12=draft}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=draft}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era|12=draft}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=draft}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era|12=draft}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi|12=draft}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era|12=draft}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja|12=draft}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=draft}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = {{ill|Nagasaki City, Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|12=draft}}, Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|12=draft}} | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a {{ill|Toshogu Shrine|ja|東照宮|lt=Toshogu Shrine|12=draft}} enshrined in {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City|12=draft}}.
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City|12=draft}}. The shrine building is built in a {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|12=draft}} style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|12=draft}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|ja|鎮西大社諏訪神社|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|12=draft}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦|lt=Hōreki era|12=draft}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun era|ja|寛文|lt=Kanbun era|12=draft}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakaue Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro|12=draft}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=draft}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|12=draft}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=draft}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|lt=Suwa Taisha|12=draft}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|lt=Kōnin era|12=draft}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|lt=Kūkai|12=draft}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Shinjūji|ja|神宮寺|lt=Shinjūji|12=draft}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗|lt=Shingon Buddhism|12=draft}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=draft}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho|12=draft}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunwa era|ja|文和|lt=Bunwa era|12=draft}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi|12=draft}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄|lt=Eiroku era|12=draft}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi|12=draft}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩|lt=Suwa Domain|12=draft}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō|12=draft}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki Magistrates|ja|長崎奉行|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates|12=draft}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|ja|勝海舟|lt=Katsu Kaishū|12=draft}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=draft}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era|12=draft}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=draft}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era|12=draft}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi|12=draft}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era|12=draft}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja|12=draft}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=draft}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = {{ill|Nagasaki City, Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|12=draft}}, Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|12=draft}} | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a {{ill|Toshogu Shrine|ja|東照宮|lt=Toshogu Shrine|12=draft}} enshrined in {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City|12=draft}}.
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nagasaki City|ja|長崎市|lt=Nagasaki City|12=draft}}. The shrine building is built in a {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|12=draft}} style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|12=draft}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|ja|鎮西大社諏訪神社|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|12=draft}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦|lt=Hōreki era|12=draft}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun era|ja|寛文|lt=Kanbun era|12=draft}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakaue Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro|12=draft}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=draft}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|12=draft}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=draft}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|lt=Suwa Taisha|12=draft}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|lt=Kōnin era|12=draft}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|lt=Kūkai|12=draft}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Shinjūji|ja|神宮寺|lt=Shinjūji|12=draft}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗|lt=Shingon Buddhism|12=draft}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=draft}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho|12=draft}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunwa era|ja|文和|lt=Bunwa era|12=draft}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi|12=draft}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄|lt=Eiroku era|12=draft}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi|12=draft}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩|lt=Suwa Domain|12=draft}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō|12=draft}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki Magistrates|ja|長崎奉行|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates|12=draft}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|ja|勝海舟|lt=Katsu Kaishū|12=draft}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=draft}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era|12=draft}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=draft}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era|12=draft}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi|12=draft}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji era|12=draft}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja|12=draft}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=draft}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://nagasakitoshogu.jp/ Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Shimbutsu Project] - A project on {{ill|Suwa faith|ja|諏訪信仰|lt=Suwa faith|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu Shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|lt=Shinbutsu Shūgō|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri|12=draft}} history.

'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Toshogu Shrine]] enshrined in [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]].
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyamayacho|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]]. The shrine building is built in a [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|Irimoya-zukuri]] style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At [[:en:Suwa Shrine (Nagasaki)|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja]], a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the [[:en:Hōreki|Hōreki era]].<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In [[:en:Kanbun (era)|Kanbun era]] 12 (1672), [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakaue Tamuramaro]] founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|qq=}} (which was abolished during the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]]). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|qq=}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa Taisha]] Upper Shrine.
During the [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin era]], under the guidance of [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), [[:en:Jingū-ji|Shinjūji]] became a temple for spreading [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|qq=}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōhō Tanrinsho|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 檀林}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In [[:en:Bunna|Bunwa era]] 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shun-Kai Hōshi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the temple site was relocated. In [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku era]] 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soncho Hōshi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the [[:en:Suwa Domain|Suwa Domain]]. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chieidō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive [[:en:Nagasaki bugyō|Nagasaki Magistrates]] and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, [[:en:Katsu Kaishū|Katsu Kaishū]], who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|qq=}} was abolished in [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|qq=}}. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadayoshi Takagi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshogu Jinja|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|qq=}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|qq=}} was abolished in [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|qq=}}. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadayoshi Takagi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshogu Jinja|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|qq=}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長崎東照宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]], [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]], Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_Rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|Irimoya-zukuri]] | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Toshogu Shrine]] enshrined in [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]].
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyamayacho|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]]. The shrine building is built in a [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|Irimoya-zukuri]] style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At [[:en:Suwa Shrine (Nagasaki)|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja]], a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the [[:en:Hōreki|Hōreki era]].<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In [[:en:Kanbun (era)|Kanbun era]] 12 (1672), [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakaue Tamuramaro]] founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|qq=|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} (which was abolished during the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]]). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|qq=|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa Taisha]] Upper Shrine.
During the [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin era]], under the guidance of [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), [[:en:Jingū-ji|Shinjūji]] became a temple for spreading [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|qq=|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōhō Tanrinsho|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 檀林|1=danrin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544577}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In [[:en:Bunna|Bunwa era]] 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shun-Kai Hōshi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the temple site was relocated. In [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku era]] 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soncho Hōshi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the [[:en:Suwa Domain|Suwa Domain]]. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chieidō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive [[:en:Nagasaki bugyō|Nagasaki Magistrates]] and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, [[:en:Katsu Kaishū|Katsu Kaishū]], who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|qq=|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} was abolished in [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|qq=|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadayoshi Takagi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshogu Jinja|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|qq=|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At [[:en:Suwa Shrine (Nagasaki)|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja]], a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the [[:en:Hōreki|Hōreki era]].<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In [[:en:Kanbun (era)|Kanbun era]] 12 (1672), [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakaue Tamuramaro]] founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} (which was abolished during the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]]). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa Taisha]] Upper Shrine.
During the [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin era]], under the guidance of [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), [[:en:Jingū-ji|Shinjūji]] became a temple for spreading [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 檀林|1=danrin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544577}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In [[:en:Bunna|Bunwa era]] 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the temple site was relocated. In [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku era]] 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soncho Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the [[:en:Suwa Domain|Suwa Domain]]. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chieidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive [[:en:Nagasaki bugyō|Nagasaki Magistrates]] and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, [[:en:Katsu Kaishū|Katsu Kaishū]], who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} was abolished in [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadayoshi Takagi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshogu Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Toshogu Shrine]] enshrined in [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]].
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyamayacho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]]. The shrine building is built in a [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|Irimoya-zukuri]] style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>

It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa Taisha]] Upper Shrine.

In [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku era]] 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soncho Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長崎東照宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]], [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]], Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_Rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|Irimoya-zukuri]] | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Toshogu Shrine]] enshrined in [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]].
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyamayacho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]]. The shrine building is built in a [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|Irimoya-zukuri]] style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At [[:en:Suwa Shrine (Nagasaki)|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja]], a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the [[:en:Hōreki|Hōreki era]].<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In [[:en:Kanbun (era)|Kanbun era]] 12 (1672), [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakaue Tamuramaro]] founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} (which was abolished during the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]]). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa Taisha]] Upper Shrine.
During the [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin era]], under the guidance of [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), [[:en:Jingū-ji|Shinjūji]] became a temple for spreading [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 檀林|1=danrin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544577}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In [[:en:Bunna|Bunwa era]] 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the temple site was relocated. In [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku era]] 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soncho Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the [[:en:Suwa Domain|Suwa Domain]]. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chieidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive [[:en:Nagasaki bugyō|Nagasaki Magistrates]] and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, [[:en:Katsu Kaishū|Katsu Kaishū]], who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} was abolished in [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadayoshi Takagi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshogu Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://nagasakitoshogu.jp/ Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Shimbutsu Project] - A project on {{ill|Suwa Shrine|lt=Suwa Shrine|lt=Suwa faith|ja|諏訪信仰|lt=Suwa Shrine|lt=Suwa faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa faith|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪神社}}, [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu Shūgō]], and [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]] history.
{{Shinto Navbox}} {{Shinto-stub}}

}}

[[ja:長崎東照宮]]
<!--Nagasaki Tōshō-gū--> [[Category:Tōshō-gū]] [[Category:Nagasaki during the Edo period]] [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki]]
[[en:Nagasaki Tōshō-gū]] {{wikidata link|Q136730796}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長崎東照宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]], [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]], Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_Rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|Irimoya-zukuri]] | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Toshogu Shrine]] enshrined in [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]].
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyamayacho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]]. The shrine building is built in a [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|Irimoya-zukuri]] style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At [[:en:Suwa Shrine (Nagasaki)|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja]], a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the [[:en:Hōreki|Hōreki era]].<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In [[:en:Kanbun (era)|Kanbun era]] 12 (1672), [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakaue Tamuramaro]] founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} (which was abolished during the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]]). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa Taisha]] Upper Shrine.
During the [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin era]], under the guidance of [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), [[:en:Jingū-ji|Shinjūji]] became a temple for spreading [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 檀林|1=danrin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544577}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In [[:en:Bunna|Bunwa era]] 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the temple site was relocated. In [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku era]] 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soncho Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the [[:en:Suwa Domain|Suwa Domain]]. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chieidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive [[:en:Nagasaki bugyō|Nagasaki Magistrates]] and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, [[:en:Katsu Kaishū|Katsu Kaishū]], who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} was abolished in [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadayoshi Takagi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshogu Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} was abolished in [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadayoshi Takagi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshogu Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長崎東照宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]], [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]], Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_Rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|Irimoya-zukuri]] | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Toshogu Shrine]] enshrined in [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]].
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyamayacho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]]. The shrine building is built in a [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|Irimoya-zukuri]] style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At [[:en:Suwa Shrine (Nagasaki)|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja]], a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the [[:en:Hōreki|Hōreki era]].<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In [[:en:Kanbun (era)|Kanbun era]] 12 (1672), [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakaue Tamuramaro]] founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} (which was abolished during the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]]). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa Taisha]] Upper Shrine.
During the [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin era]], under the guidance of [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), [[:en:Jingū-ji|Shinjūji]] became a temple for spreading [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 檀林|1=danrin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544577}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In [[:en:Bunna|Bunwa era]] 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the temple site was relocated. In [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku era]] 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soncho Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the [[:en:Suwa Domain|Suwa Domain]]. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chieidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive [[:en:Nagasaki bugyō|Nagasaki Magistrates]] and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, [[:en:Katsu Kaishū|Katsu Kaishū]], who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} was abolished in [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadayoshi Takagi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshogu Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

== History == In [[:en:Kanbun (era)|Kanbun era]] 12 (1672), [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakaue Tamuramaro]] founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} (which was abolished during the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]]). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa Taisha]] Upper Shrine.
During the [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin era]], under the guidance of [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), [[:en:Jingū-ji|Shinjūji]] became a temple for spreading [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 檀林|1=danrin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544577}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In [[:en:Bunna|Bunwa era]] 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the temple site was relocated. In [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku era]] 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soncho Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the [[:en:Suwa Domain|Suwa Domain]]. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chieidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive [[:en:Nagasaki bugyō|Nagasaki Magistrates]] and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, [[:en:Katsu Kaishū|Katsu Kaishū]], who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} was abolished in [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadayoshi Takagi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshogu Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://nagasakitoshogu.jp/ Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Shimbutsu Project] - A project on {{ill|Suwa Shrine|lt=Suwa Shrine|lt=Suwa faith|ja|諏訪信仰|lt=Suwa Shrine|lt=Suwa faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa faith|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪神社}}, [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu Shūgō]], and [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]] history.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長崎東照宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長崎東照宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長崎東照宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]], [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]], Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_Rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|Irimoya-zukuri]] | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Toshogu Shrine]] enshrined in [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]].
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyamayacho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]]. The shrine building is built in a [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|Irimoya-zukuri]] style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At [[:en:Suwa Shrine (Nagasaki)|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja]], a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the [[:en:Hōreki|Hōreki era]].<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In [[:en:Kanbun (era)|Kanbun era]] 12 (1672), [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakaue Tamuramaro]] founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} (which was abolished during the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]]). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa Taisha]] Upper Shrine.
During the [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin era]], under the guidance of [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), [[:en:Jingū-ji|Shinjūji]] became a temple for spreading [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 檀林|1=danrin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544577}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In [[:en:Bunna|Bunwa era]] 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the temple site was relocated. In [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku era]] 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soncho Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the [[:en:Suwa Domain|Suwa Domain]]. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chieidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive [[:en:Nagasaki bugyō|Nagasaki Magistrates]] and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, [[:en:Katsu Kaishū|Katsu Kaishū]], who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} was abolished in [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadayoshi Takagi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshogu Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>
