<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長崎東照宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長崎東照宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長崎東照宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]], [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]], Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_Rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|Irimoya-zukuri]] | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Toshogu Shrine]] enshrined in [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]].
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyamayacho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki City]]. The shrine building is built in a [[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|Irimoya-zukuri]] style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At [[:en:Suwa Shrine (Nagasaki)|Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja]], a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the [[:en:Hōreki|Hōreki era]].<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In [[:en:Kanbun (era)|Kanbun era]] 12 (1672), [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakaue Tamuramaro]] founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} (which was abolished during the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]]). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa Taisha]] Upper Shrine.
During the [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin era]], under the guidance of [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), [[:en:Jingū-ji|Shinjūji]] became a temple for spreading [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 檀林|1=danrin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544577}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In [[:en:Bunna|Bunwa era]] 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the temple site was relocated. In [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku era]] 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soncho Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the [[:en:Suwa Domain|Suwa Domain]]. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chieidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive [[:en:Nagasaki bugyō|Nagasaki Magistrates]] and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, [[:en:Katsu Kaishū|Katsu Kaishū]], who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} was abolished in [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadayoshi Takagi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshogu Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://nagasakitoshogu.jp/ Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Shimbutsu Project] - A project on {{ill|Suwa Shrine|lt=Suwa Shrine|lt=Suwa faith|ja|諏訪信仰|lt=Suwa Shrine|lt=Suwa faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa faith|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪神社}}, [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu Shūgō]], and [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]] history.

In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>

It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Upper Shrine.

During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|en|Katsu Kaishū|WD=Q700470|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>

After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}. In {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku era}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soncho Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|en|Suwa Domain|WD=Q3034531|lt=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chieidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki bugyō|en|Nagasaki bugyō|WD=Q3181438|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|en|Katsu Kaishū|WD=Q700470|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadayoshi Takagi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshogu Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://nagasakitoshogu.jp/ Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Shimbutsu Project] - A project on {{ill|Suwa Shrine|lt=Suwa Shrine|lt=Suwa faith|ja|諏訪信仰|lt=Suwa Shrine|lt=Suwa faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa faith|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪神社}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu Shūgō}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri}} history.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長崎東照宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長崎東照宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長崎東照宮]]
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki City}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|en|Nagasaki Prefecture|WD=Q169376|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_Rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = {{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|WD=Q855025|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a {{ill|Tōshō-gū|en|Tōshō-gū|WD=Q1190456|lt=Toshogu Shrine}} enshrined in {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki City}}.
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyamayacho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki City}}. The shrine building is built in a {{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|WD=Q855025|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Suwa Shrine (Nagasaki)|en|Suwa Shrine (Nagasaki)|WD=Q84028|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki era}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun era}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|en|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|WD=Q941979|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin (era)|en|Kōnin (era)|WD=Q1373070|lt=Kōnin era}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Jingū-ji|en|Jingū-ji|WD=Q1074971|lt=Shinjūji}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 檀林|1=danrin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544577}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunna|en|Bunna|WD=Q1146252|lt=Bunwa era}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku era}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soncho Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|en|Suwa Domain|WD=Q3034531|lt=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chieidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki bugyō|en|Nagasaki bugyō|WD=Q3181438|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|en|Katsu Kaishū|WD=Q700470|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadayoshi Takagi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshogu Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki City}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|en|Nagasaki Prefecture|WD=Q169376|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_Rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = {{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|WD=Q855025|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a {{ill|Tōshō-gū|en|Tōshō-gū|WD=Q1190456|lt=Toshogu Shrine}} enshrined in {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki City}}.
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyamayacho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki City}}. The shrine building is built in a {{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|WD=Q855025|lt=Irimoya-zukuri}} style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Suwa Shrine (Nagasaki)|en|Suwa Shrine (Nagasaki)|WD=Q84028|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki era}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun era}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|en|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|WD=Q941979|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa Taisha}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin (era)|en|Kōnin (era)|WD=Q1373070|lt=Kōnin era}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Jingū-ji|en|Jingū-ji|WD=Q1074971|lt=Shinjūji}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 檀林|1=danrin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544577}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunna|en|Bunna|WD=Q1146252|lt=Bunwa era}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku era}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soncho Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|en|Suwa Domain|WD=Q3034531|lt=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chieidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki bugyō|en|Nagasaki bugyō|WD=Q3181438|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|en|Katsu Kaishū|WD=Q700470|lt=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadayoshi Takagi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshogu Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://nagasakitoshogu.jp/ Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine] * [https://suwa-tabi.jp/feature/suwa-shinbutsupj/ Suwa Shimbutsu Project] - A project on {{ill|Suwa Shrine|lt=Suwa Shrine|lt=Suwa faith|ja|諏訪信仰|lt=Suwa Shrine|lt=Suwa faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa faith|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪神社}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu Shūgō}}, and {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri}} history.

== History == In {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun era|1=Kanbun (era)}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|en|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|WD=Q941979|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro|1=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|1=Haibutsu kishaku}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa Taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin (era)|en|Kōnin (era)|WD=Q1373070|lt=Kōnin era|1=Kōnin (era)}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Jingū-ji|en|Jingū-ji|WD=Q1074971|lt=Shinjūji|1=Jingū-ji}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism|1=Shingon Buddhism}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495027}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 檀林|1=danrin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544577}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunna|en|Bunna|WD=Q1146252|lt=Bunwa era|1=Bunna}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku era|1=Eiroku}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soncho Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|en|Suwa Domain|WD=Q3034531|lt=Suwa Domain|1=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chieidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki bugyō|en|Nagasaki bugyō|WD=Q3181438|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates|1=Nagasaki bugyō}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|en|Katsu Kaishū|WD=Q700470|lt=Katsu Kaishū|1=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17157679}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadayoshi Takagi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshogu Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長崎東照宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長崎東照宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長崎東照宮]]
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine | image = File:NagasakiToshogu.jpg|250px | location = {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|qid=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki City|1=Nagasaki}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|en|Nagasaki Prefecture|qid=Q169376|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|1=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, Kamiyamayacho 19 | iso = JP-42 | latitude_deg = | latitude_min = | latitude_sec = | longitude_deg = | longitude_min = | longitude_sec = | label_position = left | kami = | shrine_Rank = | founded = 1672 (Kanbun 12) | main_hall = {{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|qid=Q855025|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} | alias = | pilgrimage_site = | festival = | ceremonies = | map = }}
'''Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine''' (ながさきとうしょうぐう, Nagasaki Toshogu-gū<ref group=annotation>The official name is "Toshogu Shrine," and "Nagasaki Toshogu Shrine" is its common name.</ref>) is a {{ill|Tōshō-gū|en|Tōshō-gū|qid=Q1190456|lt=Toshogu Shrine|1=Tōshō-gū}} enshrined in {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|qid=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki City|1=Nagasaki}}.
Its enshrinement site is in {{ill|Kamiyamayacho|ja|上西山町|lt=Kamiyamayacho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyamayacho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|qid=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki City|1=Nagasaki}}. The shrine building is built in a {{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|qid=Q855025|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} style with three ken and one go (traditional Japanese architectural units), and features a stone torii gate at the front. In one corner of the precincts, the {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja|長崎県神社庁舎|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaki Prefecture Shinto Association building|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} has been constructed.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 133, 233.</ref>
At {{ill|Suwa Shrine (Nagasaki)|en|Suwa Shrine (Nagasaki)|qid=Q84028|lt=Chinsei Taisha Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine (Nagasaki)}}, a ritual of drawing young water from the precinct's well and offering it to Toshogu Shrine was performed annually every New Year starting from the {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|qid=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki era|1=Hōreki}}.<ref name=NagasakiToshogu/>
== History == In {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|qid=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun era|1=Kanbun (era)}} 12 (1672), {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|en|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|qid=Q941979|lt=Sakaue Tamuramaro|1=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}} founded the shrine on the night of January 16 at midnight, along with {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17157679}} (which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|qid=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|1=Haibutsu kishaku}}). It was named {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11495027}} as a branch temple (別当寺) of the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|qid=Q218813|lt=Suwa Taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine.
During the {{ill|Kōnin (era)|en|Kōnin (era)|qid=Q1373070|lt=Kōnin era|1=Kōnin (era)}}, under the guidance of {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|qid=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi), {{ill|Jingū-ji|en|Jingū-ji|qid=Q1074971|lt=Shinjūji|1=Jingū-ji}} became a temple for spreading {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism|1=Shingon Buddhism}} and {{ill|Jigan-ji|ja|慈眼寺|lt=Jigan-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigan-ji|1=Jigenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11495027}} served as an educational dojo for Shingon Buddhism, designated as a {{ill|Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja|常法談林所|lt=Jōhō Tanrinsho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōhō Tanrinsho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 檀林|1=danrin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11544577}}, and was hailed as "the foremost dojo among the four temple schools in Shinshu."
In {{ill|Bunna|en|Bunna|qid=Q1146252|lt=Bunwa era|1=Bunna}} 1 (1352), under Master {{ill|Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja|俊海法師|lt=Shun-Kai Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shun-Kai Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the temple site was relocated. In {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|qid=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku era|1=Eiroku}} 3 (1560), during Master {{ill|Soncho Hōshi|ja|尊朝法師|lt=Soncho Hōshi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soncho Hōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it moved to its current location and was renamed "Buppou Shoryuji." During the Edo period, it became a prayer temple for the {{ill|Suwa Domain|en|Suwa Domain|qid=Q3034531|lt=Suwa Domain|1=Suwa Domain}}. In the Taishō period, it encountered a great fire, destroying the main hall, kitchen, founding hall, Kongōden, and corridors, but the main hall, kitchen, and founding hall were rebuilt. In Heisei 1 (1889), {{ill|Chieidō|ja|智映堂|lt=Chieidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chieidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was established.
Subsequently, the Toshogu Shrine was revered by successive {{ill|Nagasaki bugyō|en|Nagasaki bugyō|qid=Q3181438|lt=Nagasaki Magistrates|1=Nagasaki bugyō}} and commoners. During the end of the Edo period, {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|en|Katsu Kaishū|qid=Q700470|lt=Katsu Kaishū|1=Katsu Kaishū}}, who visited the shrine, donated a wakizashi sword.<ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, p. 132.</ref>
The priests of the branch temple of Toshogu Shrine received the same treatment as the local officials of Nagasaki, being allocated ceremonial silver (地役人の給与。貿易利益や、貿易関係の諸税から配分された。)<ref group=annotation>Essentially, this refers to the salaries of local officials, distributed from trade profits and various trade-related taxes.</ref>.<ref>Maiko Tomori, "Retainers of the Edo Shogunate" Tokyo Do Publishing, p. 340.</ref>
After {{ill|Anzen-ji|ja|安禅寺|lt=Anzen-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anzen-ji|1=Anzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17157679}} was abolished in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 1, the shrine lost its managing priest and the shrine building fell into disrepair, so it was temporarily enshrined together with {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2370082}}. In {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 30 (1897), the shrine building was rebuilt, and {{ill|Tadayoshi Takagi|ja|高木忠悦|lt=Tadayoshi Takagi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadayoshi Takagi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in Nagasaki, was appointed as the managing priest. In {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), the Nagasaki General Town Assembly was held, and it was decided that the shrine would become {{ill|Toshogu Jinja|ja|東照宮神社|lt=Toshogu Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshogu Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become a branch shrine of {{ill|Suwa Jinja|ja|諏訪神社|lt=Suwa Jinja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Jinja|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2370082}}.<ref name=Anzen-ji/><ref>Harutoshi Takato, "Ieyasu and All Toshogu Shrines Nationwide" Tokyo Bijutsu, pp. 132-133.</ref>
